texts.label: text
texts.orig: If printed, copied or otherwise transferred from eLibrary, Trust -wide Clinical Guidelines will be considered 'uncontrolled copies'.  Staff must always consult the most up to date PDF version which is registered on eLibrary.
texts.text: If printed, copied or otherwise transferred from eLibrary, Trust -wide Clinical Guidelines will be considered 'uncontrolled copies'.  Staff must always consult the most up to date PDF version which is registered on eLibrary.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This guideline is intended for the renal multidisciplinary team (MDT) including consultant nephrologists, specialist registrars, specialist nurses, dietitians and pharmacists. This is a locally adapted guideline based on national recommendations and applies to patients with chronic kidney disease Stage 5 on haemodialysis (HD).
texts.text: This guideline is intended for the renal multidisciplinary team (MDT) including consultant nephrologists, specialist registrars, specialist nurses, dietitians and pharmacists. This is a locally adapted guideline based on national recommendations and applies to patients with chronic kidney disease Stage 5 on haemodialysis (HD).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: All patients should be reviewed within one month of starting HD by a renal dietitian (Renal Association, 2010). All HD patients should have a nutritional review at least every three-six months, and patients who are highlighted as being nutritional at risk may need a review more frequently.  The patient may have a review at their haemodialysis QA review or a referral may be made by the nursing team.  The renal referral criteria (Appendix A) should be used by the MDT to highlight HD patients requiring review (K/DOQI, 2000; Renal Workforce planning, 2002).
texts.text: All patients should be reviewed within one month of starting HD by a renal dietitian (Renal Association, 2010). All HD patients should have a nutritional review at least every three-six months, and patients who are highlighted as being nutritional at risk may need a review more frequently.  The patient may have a review at their haemodialysis QA review or a referral may be made by the nursing team.  The renal referral criteria (Appendix A) should be used by the MDT to highlight HD patients requiring review (K/DOQI, 2000; Renal Workforce planning, 2002).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Nutritional assessment should not rely on a single marker e.g. weight (K/DOQI, 2000) (Appendix B and C).
texts.text: Nutritional assessment should not rely on a single marker e.g. weight (K/DOQI, 2000) (Appendix B and C).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The renal dietitian will participate in the HD quality assurance (QA) sessions and HD QA clinics. These will help facilitate dietetic input and discussions surrounding patient care and treatment plans.
texts.text: The renal dietitian will participate in the HD quality assurance (QA) sessions and HD QA clinics. These will help facilitate dietetic input and discussions surrounding patient care and treatment plans.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: During the dietary assessment of HD patients the following aspects should be considered:
texts.text: During the dietary assessment of HD patients the following aspects should be considered:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The pre-dialysis  potassium  level  should  be  maintained  between  4-6mmol/l  (Renal  Association, 2010).  Dietary  potassium  intake  should  be  assessed  and  recommendations  made  to  help maintain this serum potassium level.
texts.text: The pre-dialysis  potassium  level  should  be  maintained  between  4-6mmol/l  (Renal  Association, 2010).  Dietary  potassium  intake  should  be  assessed  and  recommendations  made  to  help maintain this serum potassium level.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Aim for a phosphate intake of 1000-1400mg (EDTNA, 2002)
texts.text: Aim for a phosphate intake of 1000-1400mg (EDTNA, 2002)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Maintain pre-dialysis serum phosphate between 1.1-1.7mmol/l (Renal Association, 2010). Dietary phosphate intake should be assessed and recommendations made to help maintain this serum phosphate level without compromising protein intake.
texts.text: Maintain pre-dialysis serum phosphate between 1.1-1.7mmol/l (Renal Association, 2010). Dietary phosphate intake should be assessed and recommendations made to help maintain this serum phosphate level without compromising protein intake.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Calcium levels should be maintained at 2.10-2.5 mmol/l (corrected for serum albumin). The following phosphate binding medication is used: Calcium acetate (Renacet and Phosex), Calcium carbonate (Calcichew), Lanthanum (Fosrenol), Sevelemer hydrochloride (Renagel) and Sevelemer carbonate (Renvela). Calcium acetate should be used as first line where appropriate (NICE, 2013). Other non-dietary causes of high phosphate should be considered i.e. adequate HD.
texts.text: Calcium levels should be maintained at 2.10-2.5 mmol/l (corrected for serum albumin). The following phosphate binding medication is used: Calcium acetate (Renacet and Phosex), Calcium carbonate (Calcichew), Lanthanum (Fosrenol), Sevelemer hydrochloride (Renagel) and Sevelemer carbonate (Renvela). Calcium acetate should be used as first line where appropriate (NICE, 2013). Other non-dietary causes of high phosphate should be considered i.e. adequate HD.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Intact Parathyroid Hormone: 8 - 38 pmol/L (2-9 times the upper laboratory target)
texts.text: Intact Parathyroid Hormone: 8 - 38 pmol/L (2-9 times the upper laboratory target)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Treatment should be based on trends; patients with levels of iPTH above the target range should be assessed for hyperphosphataemia and hypocalcaemia (KDIGO, 2009).
texts.text: Treatment should be based on trends; patients with levels of iPTH above the target range should be assessed for hyperphosphataemia and hypocalcaemia (KDIGO, 2009).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This should help to improve blood pressure control and minimise fluid gains. Salt substitutes that contain potassium salts should be avoided unless advised otherwise by the dietitian (SIGN, 2008).
texts.text: This should help to improve blood pressure control and minimise fluid gains. Salt substitutes that contain potassium salts should be avoided unless advised otherwise by the dietitian (SIGN, 2008).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Fluid: Aim for 500ml + previous days urine output (EDTNA, 2002)
texts.text: Fluid: Aim for 500ml + previous days urine output (EDTNA, 2002)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Patients should be advised on a maximum weight gain of 2Kg or 4% of dry weight.
texts.text: Patients should be advised on a maximum weight gain of 2Kg or 4% of dry weight.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The dietitian will advise on ways to achieve an adequate intake of vitamins and minerals.
texts.text: The dietitian will advise on ways to achieve an adequate intake of vitamins and minerals.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: CKD- Chronic Kidney Disease HD- Haemodialysis IBW- Ideal body weight iPTH- Intact Parathyroid hormone MAC- Mid-arm circumference TSF- Tricep skinfold thickness IDWG- Intra-dialytic weight gain JVP - Jugular venous pressure SGA - Subjective globular assessment Kt/v - marker of dialysis adequacy
texts.text: CKD- Chronic Kidney Disease HD- Haemodialysis IBW- Ideal body weight iPTH- Intact Parathyroid hormone MAC- Mid-arm circumference TSF- Tricep skinfold thickness IDWG- Intra-dialytic weight gain JVP - Jugular venous pressure SGA - Subjective globular assessment Kt/v - marker of dialysis adequacy

texts.label: text
texts.orig: All renal dietitians should be aware and understand this guideline to assist with its implementation. This will ensure that all CKD HD patients that are referred to a renal dietitian are given dietary advice that is evidence based.
texts.text: All renal dietitians should be aware and understand this guideline to assist with its implementation. This will ensure that all CKD HD patients that are referred to a renal dietitian are given dietary advice that is evidence based.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The prioritisation and frequency of dietetic review will be decided by the patient's renal dietitian (NHS Commissioning Board, 2013). The frequency of monitoring may need to increase to monthly in patients who have a very poor appetite and a significant weight loss. Those stable on dialysis (stable weight and stable diet related electrolytes) may need a review every six months.
texts.text: The prioritisation and frequency of dietetic review will be decided by the patient's renal dietitian (NHS Commissioning Board, 2013). The frequency of monitoring may need to increase to monthly in patients who have a very poor appetite and a significant weight loss. Those stable on dialysis (stable weight and stable diet related electrolytes) may need a review every six months.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To achieve/maintain an IBW and nitrogen balance To minimise morbidity and mortality risk associated with malnutrition
texts.text: To achieve/maintain an IBW and nitrogen balance To minimise morbidity and mortality risk associated with malnutrition

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Energy requirement are increased in HD and CKD due to the activation of the complement cascade and cytokine release from the HD process and uraemia. This can often be complicated by a reduced appetite or nausea.
texts.text: Energy requirement are increased in HD and CKD due to the activation of the complement cascade and cytokine release from the HD process and uraemia. This can often be complicated by a reduced appetite or nausea.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  30-35kcal/kg IBW is recommended for renal patients (Renal Association , 2010)
texts.text:  30-35kcal/kg IBW is recommended for renal patients (Renal Association , 2010)

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  During acute illness energy requirements will increase further (K/DOQI, 2000)
texts.text:  During acute illness energy requirements will increase further (K/DOQI, 2000)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To maintain nitrogen balance To minimize morbidity and mortality risk associated with malnutrition
texts.text: To maintain nitrogen balance To minimize morbidity and mortality risk associated with malnutrition

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Protein requirements in HD are increased due to the activation of cytokine release and complement cascade. High flux dialysis reduces this problem but increases the removal of higher molecular weight molecules such as peptides. Consequently protein requirements are increased to compensate for losses.
texts.text: Protein requirements in HD are increased due to the activation of cytokine release and complement cascade. High flux dialysis reduces this problem but increases the removal of higher molecular weight molecules such as peptides. Consequently protein requirements are increased to compensate for losses.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  A minimum of 1.1g protein/Kg IBW is recommended for maintenance HD patients (British Dietetic Association Renal Nutrition Group, 2011)
texts.text:  A minimum of 1.1g protein/Kg IBW is recommended for maintenance HD patients (British Dietetic Association Renal Nutrition Group, 2011)

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Catabolic and patients who are acutely unwell will have increased protein requirements
texts.text:  Catabolic and patients who are acutely unwell will have increased protein requirements

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Adequate energy intake is required to achieve positive nitrogen balance (EDTNA, 2002)
texts.text:  Adequate energy intake is required to achieve positive nitrogen balance (EDTNA, 2002)

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  At least 50% of the recommended protein intake should be of a HBV (High Biological Value) (K/DOQI, 2000)
texts.text:  At least 50% of the recommended protein intake should be of a HBV (High Biological Value) (K/DOQI, 2000)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Aim: Renal Association standards recommend pre-dialysis serum potassium levels are maintained between 4-6 mmol/l
texts.text: Aim: Renal Association standards recommend pre-dialysis serum potassium levels are maintained between 4-6 mmol/l

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Where serum potassium levels are elevated non dietary causes of raised potassium should be considered before dietary restriction (Appendix D)
texts.text:  Where serum potassium levels are elevated non dietary causes of raised potassium should be considered before dietary restriction (Appendix D)

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Aim for a normal range of serum bicarbonate (22-29mmol/l)
texts.text:  Aim for a normal range of serum bicarbonate (22-29mmol/l)

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Where the serum potassium is elevated and catabolism has been excluded, the patient should be advised on a potassium intake of 1mmol/Kg/BW (EDTNA, 2002 )
texts.text:  Where the serum potassium is elevated and catabolism has been excluded, the patient should be advised on a potassium intake of 1mmol/Kg/BW (EDTNA, 2002 )

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Where pre-dialysis serum potassium levels are consistently <3.7 mmol/l an increased potassium diet should be advised (agreed best practice). The option of using dialysis fluid higher in potassium can be discussed with the medical/nursing team
texts.text:  Where pre-dialysis serum potassium levels are consistently <3.7 mmol/l an increased potassium diet should be advised (agreed best practice). The option of using dialysis fluid higher in potassium can be discussed with the medical/nursing team

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Where pre dialysis serum potassium levels are continuously <4.5mmol/l relaxation of dietary restriction may be advised with caution (agreed best practice)
texts.text:  Where pre dialysis serum potassium levels are continuously <4.5mmol/l relaxation of dietary restriction may be advised with caution (agreed best practice)

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Patients referred with hyperkalaemia, who have not previously been advised on a low potassium diet, should be reviewed within one working day of referral (departmental standards) or when they next dialyse.
texts.text:  Patients referred with hyperkalaemia, who have not previously been advised on a low potassium diet, should be reviewed within one working day of referral (departmental standards) or when they next dialyse.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Aim: To maintain serum phosphate levels between 1.1-1.7mmol/l (Renal Association, 2010)
texts.text: Aim: To maintain serum phosphate levels between 1.1-1.7mmol/l (Renal Association, 2010)

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Calcium levels should be maintained at 2.10-2.5 mmol/l (corrected for serum albumin)
texts.text:  Calcium levels should be maintained at 2.10-2.5 mmol/l (corrected for serum albumin)

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  The dose of calcium based phosphate binders and or vitamin D analogues should be restricted in the presence or persistent or recurrent hypercalaemia (KDIGO, 2009)
texts.text:  The dose of calcium based phosphate binders and or vitamin D analogues should be restricted in the presence or persistent or recurrent hypercalaemia (KDIGO, 2009)

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Where serum phosphate levels exceeds 1.7mmol/l, other non-dietary causes will be taken into account such as dialysis adequacy, Urea reduction ratio(URR), and medications prescribed, calcium and iPTH results
texts.text:  Where serum phosphate levels exceeds 1.7mmol/l, other non-dietary causes will be taken into account such as dialysis adequacy, Urea reduction ratio(URR), and medications prescribed, calcium and iPTH results

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Unless serum phosphate is low the patient should be advised on how to achieve a phosphate intake of 1000-1400mg (agreed best practice). It may be difficult in some patients, especially vegetarians, to achieve an adequate protein intake on a low phosphate diet and this must be taken into account
texts.text:  Unless serum phosphate is low the patient should be advised on how to achieve a phosphate intake of 1000-1400mg (agreed best practice). It may be difficult in some patients, especially vegetarians, to achieve an adequate protein intake on a low phosphate diet and this must be taken into account

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Patients should be advised on the appropriate timing of phosphate binders with protein foods. Oral iron tablets should not be taken at the same time as phosphate binders as this reduces their effectiveness (Refer to phosphate binders diet sheet on the e-library)
texts.text:  Patients should be advised on the appropriate timing of phosphate binders with protein foods. Oral iron tablets should not be taken at the same time as phosphate binders as this reduces their effectiveness (Refer to phosphate binders diet sheet on the e-library)

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  The phosphate binders used locally are initially calcium acetate (Renacet and Phosex), Calcium carbonate (Calcichew), Lanthanum (Fosrenol), Sevelemer hydrochloride (Renagel) and Sevelamer Carbonate (Renvela). Calcium acetate should be used as first line where appropriate (NICE, 2013)
texts.text:  The phosphate binders used locally are initially calcium acetate (Renacet and Phosex), Calcium carbonate (Calcichew), Lanthanum (Fosrenol), Sevelemer hydrochloride (Renagel) and Sevelamer Carbonate (Renvela). Calcium acetate should be used as first line where appropriate (NICE, 2013)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: (v)   Intact Parathyroid Hormone: 8 - 38 pmol/L (2-9 times the upper laboratory target)
texts.text: (v)   Intact Parathyroid Hormone: 8 - 38 pmol/L (2-9 times the upper laboratory target)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Treatment should be based on trends; patients with levels of iPTH above the target range should be assessed for hyperphosphataemia and hypocalcaemia (KDIGO, 2009).  Active vitamin D analogues calcitriol and alfacalcidol should be used to down regulate the iPTH (KDIGO, 2009; RA, 2010). Patients with an iPTH above the target range despite appropriate use of calcitrol or alfacalcidol should be prescribed paricalcitol. Patients with a iPTH above 85pmol/L who are refractory to standard therapy (calcitriol, alfacalcidol and paricalcitol) should be considered for cinacalcet or if appropriate partial or total parathyroidectomy (NICE, 2009).
texts.text: Treatment should be based on trends; patients with levels of iPTH above the target range should be assessed for hyperphosphataemia and hypocalcaemia (KDIGO, 2009).  Active vitamin D analogues calcitriol and alfacalcidol should be used to down regulate the iPTH (KDIGO, 2009; RA, 2010). Patients with an iPTH above the target range despite appropriate use of calcitrol or alfacalcidol should be prescribed paricalcitol. Patients with a iPTH above 85pmol/L who are refractory to standard therapy (calcitriol, alfacalcidol and paricalcitol) should be considered for cinacalcet or if appropriate partial or total parathyroidectomy (NICE, 2009).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Aim: Control of Hypertension Support of fluid management
texts.text: Aim: Control of Hypertension Support of fluid management

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Dietary salt intakes >6g/d precipitate hypertension.  Aim 100mmol/6g salt a day
texts.text:  Dietary salt intakes >6g/d precipitate hypertension.  Aim 100mmol/6g salt a day

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Reducing dietary salt intake and sodium dialysate concentration has been shown to reduce blood pressure (McMahon et al, 2015)
texts.text:  Reducing dietary salt intake and sodium dialysate concentration has been shown to reduce blood pressure (McMahon et al, 2015)

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  High salt intakes are associated with an increase in thirst, which can make it very difficult to follow a fluid restriction
texts.text:  High salt intakes are associated with an increase in thirst, which can make it very difficult to follow a fluid restriction

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Where patient's nutritional intake is poor, sodium restriction may compound the problem and is not advised (agreed best practice)
texts.text:  Where patient's nutritional intake is poor, sodium restriction may compound the problem and is not advised (agreed best practice)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Aim: To help maintain an even fluid balance and control IDWG's
texts.text: Aim: To help maintain an even fluid balance and control IDWG's

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Patients should be advised on an appropriate level of IDWG. Large fluid weight gains are associated with symptomatic dialysis sessions and left ventricular hypertrophy (LVH)
texts.text:  Patients should be advised on an appropriate level of IDWG. Large fluid weight gains are associated with symptomatic dialysis sessions and left ventricular hypertrophy (LVH)

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Patients should be advised to have a fluid intake of 500mls plus the equivalent of the previous day's urine output
texts.text:  Patients should be advised to have a fluid intake of 500mls plus the equivalent of the previous day's urine output

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Maximum of 2kg or no more than 4% of dry weight/IBW (whichever is the lowest weight)
texts.text:  Maximum of 2kg or no more than 4% of dry weight/IBW (whichever is the lowest weight)

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Changes in dry weight are an important consideration in the review of HD patients. Such changes are often subtle and difficult to identify. The dietitian should be informed if patients are losing dry weight since intervention is required
texts.text:  Changes in dry weight are an important consideration in the review of HD patients. Such changes are often subtle and difficult to identify. The dietitian should be informed if patients are losing dry weight since intervention is required

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Methods of monitoring dry weight include:
texts.text:  Methods of monitoring dry weight include:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Medical/ nursing assessment: JVP, chest x-ray, pre and post dialysis blood pressure,
texts.text:  Medical/ nursing assessment: JVP, chest x-ray, pre and post dialysis blood pressure,

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: haematocrit monitoring, assessing for oedema and Crit-line on HD
texts.text: haematocrit monitoring, assessing for oedema and Crit-line on HD

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Aim: To improve intake of vitamins and minerals
texts.text: Aim: To improve intake of vitamins and minerals

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The dietitian will advise on ways to achieve an adequate intake of vitamins and minerals is adequate.
texts.text: The dietitian will advise on ways to achieve an adequate intake of vitamins and minerals is adequate.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Water-soluble vitamins are routinely given to HD patients on the renal unit post dialysis, 2-3 x/week. These are: ascorbic acid (100mg), vitamin B compound strong (containing 20mg nicotinamide, 2mg pyridoxine, 2mg riboflavin and 4.85mg thiamine) and folic acid 5mg.This level of supplementation is designed to make up any shortfalls from a low potassium diet, and replace any potential losses on dialysis. There is insufficient research to set a definitive reference nutrient intake (RNI) for dialysis patients
texts.text:  Water-soluble vitamins are routinely given to HD patients on the renal unit post dialysis, 2-3 x/week. These are: ascorbic acid (100mg), vitamin B compound strong (containing 20mg nicotinamide, 2mg pyridoxine, 2mg riboflavin and 4.85mg thiamine) and folic acid 5mg.This level of supplementation is designed to make up any shortfalls from a low potassium diet, and replace any potential losses on dialysis. There is insufficient research to set a definitive reference nutrient intake (RNI) for dialysis patients

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  The Renal Association (2010) recommend that HD patients are prescribed water soluble vitamins. However no guidance is given on dose or frequency of water soluble vitamins prescribed
texts.text:  The Renal Association (2010) recommend that HD patients are prescribed water soluble vitamins. However no guidance is given on dose or frequency of water soluble vitamins prescribed

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  As in all renal patients it is recommended that HD patients do not have supplements containing fat soluble vitamins, or take fish liver oil supplements due to risks of hypervitaminosis with fat soluble vitamins (Floege et al, 2010). Suitable multivitamin preparations include Renavit and also standard pregnancy multivitamins
texts.text:  As in all renal patients it is recommended that HD patients do not have supplements containing fat soluble vitamins, or take fish liver oil supplements due to risks of hypervitaminosis with fat soluble vitamins (Floege et al, 2010). Suitable multivitamin preparations include Renavit and also standard pregnancy multivitamins

texts.label: text
texts.orig: NICE (2014) recommend the diagnosis and treatment of native vitamin D deficiency and insufficiency in those with a GFR <30mls/min. This follows on from European guideline recommendations which recommend baseline measurement of vitamin D levels, and repletion if required, in all stages of chronic kidney disease (KDIGO, 2009). It aids management of hyperparathyroidism, promotes bone mineralisation and plays an important role in muscle function (Holick, 2007; Bischoff-Ferrari , 2012).
texts.text: NICE (2014) recommend the diagnosis and treatment of native vitamin D deficiency and insufficiency in those with a GFR <30mls/min. This follows on from European guideline recommendations which recommend baseline measurement of vitamin D levels, and repletion if required, in all stages of chronic kidney disease (KDIGO, 2009). It aids management of hyperparathyroidism, promotes bone mineralisation and plays an important role in muscle function (Holick, 2007; Bischoff-Ferrari , 2012).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Vitamin D supplementation to maintain serum 25(OH)D ≥75nmol/L is recommended for all haemodialysis patients. The supplementation protocol is outlined in the separate guideline 'Vitamin D Supplementation in Haemodialysis Patients' available on the elibrary
texts.text:  Vitamin D supplementation to maintain serum 25(OH)D ≥75nmol/L is recommended for all haemodialysis patients. The supplementation protocol is outlined in the separate guideline 'Vitamin D Supplementation in Haemodialysis Patients' available on the elibrary

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: (ix) Dialysis adequacy and changes in regime.
texts.text: (ix) Dialysis adequacy and changes in regime.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Aim URR >65% for those patients dialysing three times per week (Renal Association, 2009)
texts.text:  Aim URR >65% for those patients dialysing three times per week (Renal Association, 2009)

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Aim URR >85% for those patients dialysing two times per week. Twice weekly HD is not recommended unless there is good preservation of residual renal function (Renal Association, 2007)
texts.text:  Aim URR >85% for those patients dialysing two times per week. Twice weekly HD is not recommended unless there is good preservation of residual renal function (Renal Association, 2007)

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Some patients dialyse at home on NX stage dialysis. This is more frequent dialysis for approximately 3 hours five times a week. Adequate clearance on NX stage dialysis is Kt/V > 2.2
texts.text:  Some patients dialyse at home on NX stage dialysis. This is more frequent dialysis for approximately 3 hours five times a week. Adequate clearance on NX stage dialysis is Kt/V > 2.2

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Inadequate dialysis leads to poor appetite, malnutrition and increased morbidity and mortality (Renal Association, 2010)
texts.text:  Inadequate dialysis leads to poor appetite, malnutrition and increased morbidity and mortality (Renal Association, 2010)

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Dialysis adequacy is affected by the following: kidney size, pump speeds, access type, effective needle spacing (to minimize the problem of 'recirculation') and hours on dialysis. Discuss with nursing & medical staff if patient appears to be under dialysed
texts.text:  Dialysis adequacy is affected by the following: kidney size, pump speeds, access type, effective needle spacing (to minimize the problem of 'recirculation') and hours on dialysis. Discuss with nursing & medical staff if patient appears to be under dialysed

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Nutritional support should be considered where patients are not achieving adequate nutrition (K/DOQI, 2000)
texts.text:  Nutritional support should be considered where patients are not achieving adequate nutrition (K/DOQI, 2000)

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Dietitians should ensure patients are not following any unnecessary dietary restrictions
texts.text:  Dietitians should ensure patients are not following any unnecessary dietary restrictions

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Where it is not possible to achieve adequate nutrition through food fortification, supplements should be considered
texts.text:  Where it is not possible to achieve adequate nutrition through food fortification, supplements should be considered

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  The composition of supplements should be assessed on an individual basis
texts.text:  The composition of supplements should be assessed on an individual basis

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Giving nutrition supplements on dialysis, and at home if needed, has been shown to be beneficial due to the catabolic nature of the process. These patients should be monitored regularly (at least once every three months)
texts.text:  Giving nutrition supplements on dialysis, and at home if needed, has been shown to be beneficial due to the catabolic nature of the process. These patients should be monitored regularly (at least once every three months)

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Anthropometric markers such as; hand grip, MAC and TSF are useful markers of nutrition. They should be performed regularly in HD patients particularly those considered to be at risk nutritionally (see Appendix B)
texts.text:  Anthropometric markers such as; hand grip, MAC and TSF are useful markers of nutrition. They should be performed regularly in HD patients particularly those considered to be at risk nutritionally (see Appendix B)

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  HD patients should be screened for undernutrition initially, and at 12 months using the SGA assessment tool (Renal Association, 2010)
texts.text:  HD patients should be screened for undernutrition initially, and at 12 months using the SGA assessment tool (Renal Association, 2010)

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Nasogastric or gastrostomy feeding may be suitable in some cases, and patients should be monitored at least once a month
texts.text:  Nasogastric or gastrostomy feeding may be suitable in some cases, and patients should be monitored at least once a month

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  When patients are reviewed outcomes should be discussed with the relevant nurse and documented in the nursing kardex in the nutritional care plan or medical notes for inpatients
texts.text:  When patients are reviewed outcomes should be discussed with the relevant nurse and documented in the nursing kardex in the nutritional care plan or medical notes for inpatients

texts.orig: These guidelines have been disseminated and implemented through the dietetic and renal QIPPS meetings, and are the e-library so that all members of the renal multi-disciplinary team has assess to them.
texts.text: These guidelines have been disseminated and implemented through the dietetic and renal QIPPS meetings, and are the e-library so that all members of the renal multi-disciplinary team has assess to them.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Clinical Guidelines assist in decision making; they do not replace clinical judgement. Regardless of the strength of evidence, it remains the responsibility of the clinician to interpret the application of the clinical guidance to local circumstances and the needs and wishes of the individual patient. Where variations of any kind do occur, it is important to document the variations and the reason for them in the patient's health record.
texts.text: Clinical Guidelines assist in decision making; they do not replace clinical judgement. Regardless of the strength of evidence, it remains the responsibility of the clinician to interpret the application of the clinical guidance to local circumstances and the needs and wishes of the individual patient. Where variations of any kind do occur, it is important to document the variations and the reason for them in the patient's health record.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Throughout its activities, the Trust will seek to treat all people equally and fairly.  This includes those seeking and using the services, employees and potential employees.  No-one will receive less favourable treatment on the grounds of sex/gender (including Trans People), disability, marital status, race/colour/ethnicity/nationally, sexual orientation, age, social status, their trade union activities, religion/beliefs or caring responsibilities nor will they be disadvantaged by conditions or requirements which cannot be shown to be justifiable.  All staff, whether part time, full-time, temporary, job share or volunteer; service users and partners will be treated fairly and with dignity and respect.
texts.text: Throughout its activities, the Trust will seek to treat all people equally and fairly.  This includes those seeking and using the services, employees and potential employees.  No-one will receive less favourable treatment on the grounds of sex/gender (including Trans People), disability, marital status, race/colour/ethnicity/nationally, sexual orientation, age, social status, their trade union activities, religion/beliefs or caring responsibilities nor will they be disadvantaged by conditions or requirements which cannot be shown to be justifiable.  All staff, whether part time, full-time, temporary, job share or volunteer; service users and partners will be treated fairly and with dignity and respect.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Out patient referral criteria for renal dietetic review
texts.text: Out patient referral criteria for renal dietetic review

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Please refer the following patients for dietetic review:
texts.text: Please refer the following patients for dietetic review:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If patients have not been seen by a dietitian since starting dialysis. If blood results are outside of the following and this is not a longstanding problem:
texts.text: If patients have not been seen by a dietitian since starting dialysis. If blood results are outside of the following and this is not a longstanding problem:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Poor appetite, i.e. eating less than half of normal intake
texts.text: Poor appetite, i.e. eating less than half of normal intake

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Excessive weight gain/ needs to lose weight for transplant
texts.text: Excessive weight gain/ needs to lose weight for transplant

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Attending clinic with family or carers who also need to be present during consultation.
texts.text: Attending clinic with family or carers who also need to be present during consultation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Home HD patients at clinic appointment
texts.text: Home HD patients at clinic appointment

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Patients have gained unwanted extra weight - BMI >30
texts.text: Patients have gained unwanted extra weight - BMI >30

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Stage 4-5 CKD, and any of the following:
texts.text: Stage 4-5 CKD, and any of the following:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Uraemic symptoms, including poor appetite or weight loss
texts.text: Uraemic symptoms, including poor appetite or weight loss

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Attending clinic with family or carers who also need to be present during consultation Poor diabetic control HbA1c >64mmol/mol Hypertensive
texts.text: Attending clinic with family or carers who also need to be present during consultation Poor diabetic control HbA1c >64mmol/mol Hypertensive

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Contact us on 024 7696 6151, or ext 26151 at UHCW NHS Trust
texts.text: Contact us on 024 7696 6151, or ext 26151 at UHCW NHS Trust

texts.label: text
texts.orig: (For peritoneal dialysis patients (PD) at UHCW NHS Trust this is the weight including the fluid from the bag)
texts.text: (For peritoneal dialysis patients (PD) at UHCW NHS Trust this is the weight including the fluid from the bag)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: (For HD patients this is the weight set by medical staff where JVP and chest x-ray are clear and the patient has no oedema)
texts.text: (For HD patients this is the weight set by medical staff where JVP and chest x-ray are clear and the patient has no oedema)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Ideal body weight (IBW) is equivalent to:
texts.text: Ideal body weight (IBW) is equivalent to:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: BMI of 20 if BMI is less than 20
texts.text: BMI of 20 if BMI is less than 20

texts.label: text
texts.orig: BMI of 25 if BMI is greater than 25
texts.text: BMI of 25 if BMI is greater than 25

texts.label: text
texts.orig: (Renal Association Guidelines, 2010 - Screening for undernutrition in CKD)
texts.text: (Renal Association Guidelines, 2010 - Screening for undernutrition in CKD)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Mid-arm-circumference (MAC), grip strength and skin fold thickness.
texts.text: Mid-arm-circumference (MAC), grip strength and skin fold thickness.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This should be used in those patients felt to be nutritionally at risk, assessment of dry weight for those with oedema/ on dialysis. Repeat measurements on a 3 monthly basis to highlight any changes
texts.text: This should be used in those patients felt to be nutritionally at risk, assessment of dry weight for those with oedema/ on dialysis. Repeat measurements on a 3 monthly basis to highlight any changes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Remember to document this on the appropriate yellow card inside the record card and record which arm is measured
texts.text: Remember to document this on the appropriate yellow card inside the record card and record which arm is measured

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Relevant Biochemistry (i.e those specified on blood card), and cholesterol, triglycerides, HbA1C, glucose, CRP and magnesium when available.
texts.text: Relevant Biochemistry (i.e those specified on blood card), and cholesterol, triglycerides, HbA1C, glucose, CRP and magnesium when available.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Renal Association Guidelines 2010; Screening for undernutrition in CKD
texts.text: Renal Association Guidelines 2010; Screening for undernutrition in CKD

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Screening methods for undernutrition in CKD
texts.text: Screening methods for undernutrition in CKD

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We recommend that all patients with stage 4-5 CKD should have the following parameters measured as a minimum in order to identify undernutrition (1C):
texts.text: We recommend that all patients with stage 4-5 CKD should have the following parameters measured as a minimum in order to identify undernutrition (1C):

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Actual Body Weight (ABW) (< 85% of Ideal Body Weight (IBW))
texts.text:  Actual Body Weight (ABW) (< 85% of Ideal Body Weight (IBW))

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Reduction in oedema free body weight (of 5% or more in 3 months or 10% or more in 6 months)
texts.text:  Reduction in oedema free body weight (of 5% or more in 3 months or 10% or more in 6 months)

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Subjective Global Assessment (SGA) (B/C on 3 point scale or 1-5 on 7 point scale)
texts.text:  Subjective Global Assessment (SGA) (B/C on 3 point scale or 1-5 on 7 point scale)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The above simple audit measures have been linked to increased mortality and other adverse outcomes.
texts.text: The above simple audit measures have been linked to increased mortality and other adverse outcomes.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Appendix D:  Factors affecting serum potassium levels in renal failure
texts.text: Appendix D:  Factors affecting serum potassium levels in renal failure

texts.label: text
texts.orig: An INCREASE in serum potassium could be due to :
texts.text: An INCREASE in serum potassium could be due to :

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: o Poor diabetic control / Insulin deficiency
texts.text: o Poor diabetic control / Insulin deficiency

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: o Regulan treatment - Original flavour version contains 6 - 7 mmol  k+ per sachet,  the orange and lemon/lime flavour versions contains < 1 mmol  k+ per sachet
texts.text: o Regulan treatment - Original flavour version contains 6 - 7 mmol  k+ per sachet,  the orange and lemon/lime flavour versions contains < 1 mmol  k+ per sachet

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: o Fybogel - The original flavour contains approximately 2.5mmol k+ per sachget, the orange flavour contains 0.7mmol k+ per sachet
texts.text: o Fybogel - The original flavour contains approximately 2.5mmol k+ per sachget, the orange flavour contains 0.7mmol k+ per sachet

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: o ACE inhibitors, for example; Captopril, Enalopril, Lisinopril
texts.text: o ACE inhibitors, for example; Captopril, Enalopril, Lisinopril

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: o ARBs, for example losartan, candesartan, irbesartan
texts.text: o ARBs, for example losartan, candesartan, irbesartan

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: o Potassium salts for example Slow K (occasionally used where levels are low. Renal function changes and someone forgets to stop them)
texts.text: o Potassium salts for example Slow K (occasionally used where levels are low. Renal function changes and someone forgets to stop them)

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: o Potassium sparing diuretics, eg. Spironolactone etc. (Not usually used in renal failure for this reason)
texts.text: o Potassium sparing diuretics, eg. Spironolactone etc. (Not usually used in renal failure for this reason)

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: o Constipation (more potassium is generally excreted in the stools of renal patients)
texts.text: o Constipation (more potassium is generally excreted in the stools of renal patients)

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: o A small temporary increase in potassium of 0.5 mmol/l is usual following exercise
texts.text: o A small temporary increase in potassium of 0.5 mmol/l is usual following exercise

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  In HD patients, other factors to consider
texts.text:  In HD patients, other factors to consider

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. Dextrose and insulin.  This works by moving potassium into the cells; temporary measure
texts.text: Dextrose and insulin.  This works by moving potassium into the cells; temporary measure

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 4. Ion exchanges resins e.g. calcium resonium.  This binds potassium in the gut and removes it via the stools.  The usual dose is 15g orally 3 or 4 times daily in water or 30g rectally
texts.text: Ion exchanges resins e.g. calcium resonium.  This binds potassium in the gut and removes it via the stools.  The usual dose is 15g orally 3 or 4 times daily in water or 30g rectally

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: A DECREASE in serum potassium could be due to:
texts.text: A DECREASE in serum potassium could be due to:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: o Diuretics that cause potassium loss e.g. frusemide (note:  potassium levels will rise when patient ceases these)
texts.text: o Diuretics that cause potassium loss e.g. frusemide (note:  potassium levels will rise when patient ceases these)

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: o Salbutamol - orally or high dose inhaled
texts.text: o Salbutamol - orally or high dose inhaled

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: o Amphotericin - damages the renal tubule and causes potassium loss from the body
texts.text: o Amphotericin - damages the renal tubule and causes potassium loss from the body

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: o Sodium bicarbonate treatment lowers potassium
texts.text: o Sodium bicarbonate treatment lowers potassium

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: o Recovery of renal function while maintaining reduced dietary intake
texts.text: o Recovery of renal function while maintaining reduced dietary intake
